title: Secret-detection regex forked across languages (farm.ts vs _hooklib.SECRET_RE), synced by a comment and already drifted
evidence: farm.ts:267 defines SECRET_LINE = /(api[_-]?key|token|secret|password|BEGIN.*PRIVATE|sk-ant|AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}|ghp_[A-Za-z0-9]{36})/i with a comment (264-266) that it is 'kept in step with the hook-side SECRET_RE so the outbound redactor and the commit gate never disagree on what a secret looks like'. The hook-side _hooklib.SECRET_RE (56-64) is a different pattern: it lists more keywords (apikey, private_key, passphrase, credential, aws_secret_access_key, client_secret) AND gates on a quoted-value form ("['\"]?\\s*[:=]\\s*['\"][^'\"]{4,}"), while farm.ts matches bare trigger words and adds BEGIN.*PRIVATE. The two have ALREADY diverged on both keyword set and match shape. The only synchronization mechanism is the prose comment; there is no shared test fixture run against both.
impact: The outbound redactor (which strips secrets from worktree content before it crosses to a third-party HTTPS endpoint, farm.ts redactSecrets) and the commit-time secret gate (H-10/H-10b) classify 'what is a secret' by two independently-maintained patterns. A future edit to one will not propagate to the other; a class of secret caught by the commit gate but missed by the redactor would be transmitted off-host (or vice versa). This is a security boundary maintained twice by hand.
recommendation: Treat the secret-pattern set as a single specification. At minimum add a shared test fixture (a list of must-match / must-not-match strings) exercised by BOTH the Python _hooklib test (.github/scripts/test_hooklib.py already has SECRET_RE cases) and a farm vitest, so any divergence fails CI. Better: generate or load the pattern from one source of truth. Until then, the 'kept in step' comment is false and should be corrected to state the patterns are deliberately distinct.
acceptance_criteria: A single shared fixture of secret/non-secret strings is asserted against both _hooklib.SECRET_RE and farm.ts SECRET_LINE
CI fails if the two classifiers disagree on any fixture string
The farm.ts comment accurately describes the relationship between the two patterns

title: Container hardening-flag block duplicated between run.ts and claude-inside.ts — security-load-bearing argv that must stay in sync
evidence: buildRunArgs (run.ts:127-159) emits the isolation argv: --user 1000:1000, --read-only, --tmpfs /tmp, --cap-drop ALL, --security-opt no-new-privileges, --pids-limit 512, --memory 4g, --cpus 2. buildClaudeRunArgs (claude-inside.ts:262-303) re-emits the same flag sequence verbatim, with comments admitting it ('Non-root + drop every capability: the SAME structural lockdown as run.ts', 282-283; 'the same structural lockdown as run.ts', 286-287). claude-inside.ts imports SANDBOX_USER from run.ts (43) but re-declares SANDBOX_LABEL locally (64, duplicating run.ts:44) and hand-rolls the whole flag list rather than sharing a helper. There is no extracted hardeningFlags() function the two paths call.
impact: These flags ARE the sandbox isolation guarantee. The token-bearing --with-claude box (which also injects an OAuth secret) relies on matching run.ts's lockdown. If a future hardening flag is added to run.ts (a new --cap-drop, a seccomp/--security-opt profile, a stricter --pids-limit) but not mirrored into claude-inside.ts, the credential-bearing box silently becomes the weaker of the two — the exact box where weakening matters most. The duplication is invisible to a reviewer changing only one file.
recommendation: Extract the shared hardening argv into one exported helper in run.ts (e.g. hardeningFlags(): string[] returning the --user/--read-only/--cap-drop/--security-opt/--pids-limit/--memory/--cpus block) and have buildRunArgs and buildClaudeRunArgs both splice it. Share SANDBOX_LABEL from run.ts rather than re-declaring it. A single negative test should assert both argv builders contain the full hardening set.
acceptance_criteria: The isolation flag block exists in exactly one place and both buildRunArgs and buildClaudeRunArgs consume it
SANDBOX_LABEL is defined once and imported by claude-inside.ts
A test asserts the --with-claude argv contains every hardening flag the ordinary sandbox argv does

title: farm.ts is a god-module bundling five independent subsystems behind one 1690-LOC file
evidence: farm.ts holds at least five concerns that change for independent reasons and each warrant their own module: (1) secret redaction / PEM-span / filename-denylist (213-413, ~200 LOC, a security boundary); (2) a mutation-testing engine — generateMutants with ~15 operator rules, sampling, budget, external command (767-948, ~180 LOC); (3) an OpenAI-compatible HTTP client with retry/backoff (549-614); (4) entitlement pre-screen + canary benchmarking model-selection tooling (1346-1485); (5) the DAG scheduler with scope-aware readiness + circuit breaker (1487-1639). The author has already begun extracting seams (the Worker interface ~685, RunTaskDeps ~995), evidence the monolith is recognized as hard to test in isolation. The repo's own coding-standards.md (24-27) mandates 'keep entry points thin; keep testable logic in the lib' for the Python side; the TS dispatcher violates the spirit of that with no lib split.
impact: Any change to one subsystem (e.g. adding a mutation operator) forces a reviewer across 1690 lines to rule out blast radius into the secret redactor and the git merge/staging protocol. Unit-testing the mutation engine or the redactor in isolation requires importing the whole dispatcher. The security-critical redactor shares a file with experimental model-selection tooling, raising the chance an unrelated edit perturbs it.
recommendation: Extract the two cleanest, highest-value seams first: the secret-redaction boundary (213-413) into a redact.ts and the mutation-testing engine (767-948) into a mutation.ts, each with its own focused test file. Leave the dispatch DAG, prompt building, and API client in farm.ts. This is a refactor (behavior-preserving) — route via /ca:refactor with the existing vitest suite as the parity proof.
acceptance_criteria: Secret redaction lives in its own module with a dedicated test
The mutation-testing engine lives in its own module with a dedicated test
farm.ts imports both; the existing vitest suite passes unchanged (behavioral parity)

title: Append-only-log and ADR-decisions path patterns triplicated across pre-write/pre-edit/pre-bash instead of centralized in _hooklib
evidence: The set of append-only audit files is encoded as an inline regex literal '\\.codearbiter/(?:(?:overrides|triage)\\.log|sprint-log\\.md)$' identically in pre-write.py:30 and pre-edit.py:35, and again as LOG_NAMES = '(?:(?:overrides|triage)\\.log|sprint-log\\.md)' in pre-bash.py:72. The ADR-decisions path '\\.codearbiter/decisions/.+\\.md$' is duplicated in pre-write.py:37 and pre-edit.py:53 (pre-bash.py:83 has its own DECISIONS variant). _hooklib.py already centralizes exactly this category of cross-hook detection constant (CRYPTO_RE, SECRET_RE, MIGRATION_DEFAULT_GLOBS, is_migration_path) with a stated 'so the two never drift' rationale — but the audit-log and ADR path sets were left inline. The comments betray the manual sync burden: pre-bash.py:70 notes 'sprint-log.md joins overrides.log/triage.log', i.e. sprint-log.md had to be added by hand to every copy.
impact: Adding or renaming an append-only artifact (sprint-log.md was one such addition) requires editing 3+ files; missing one silently opens a guard hole — e.g. an append-only file protected by the Write hook but not the Bash-redirect hook could be truncated via shell. This is the precise drift the existing _hooklib centralization was created to prevent, applied inconsistently.
recommendation: Lift the two path sets into _hooklib.py as the single source — e.g. AUDIT_LOG_RE / is_audit_log(rel) and DECISIONS_PATH_RE / is_decisions_path(rel) — and import them in pre-write.py, pre-edit.py, and pre-bash.py (pre-bash composes LOG_NAMES from the same constant). Mirrors how CRYPTO_RE/SECRET_RE are already shared.
acceptance_criteria: The append-only-log path set is defined once in _hooklib and imported by all three pre-* hooks
The decisions-path set is defined once in _hooklib and imported by pre-write/pre-edit
Adding a new append-only artifact requires editing exactly one location; existing guard tests still pass

title: statusline.py hand-rolls frontmatter()/arbiter-enabled parsing instead of reusing _hooklib.frontmatter_enabled
evidence: statusline.py:339 defines a local frontmatter(path) that re-implements the same contract _hooklib.frontmatter_enabled (86-108) encodes: leading-BOM tolerance, '---' opening delimiter on line 1, and the requirement that the block be CLOSED by a second '---' before keys count ('don't honor keys parsed to EOF', statusline.py:359-361, matching _hooklib's 'opened but never closed -> malformed'). arbiter_state (372-376) then checks fm.get('arbiter')=='enabled' — duplicating _hooklib.arbiter_active. statusline.py already does sys.path.insert (56) and imports _taskboardlib (58), so the shared-lib mechanism is demonstrably available; _hooklib was simply not reused here.
impact: The 'is this repo arbiter-enabled' activation contract is now read two ways. The frontmatter format already changed once (the closed-block/malformed distinction); a future change to _hooklib's parser would not reach the statusline, so the box could report a repo as enabled/disabled out of step with what the enforcement hooks actually do.
recommendation: Import frontmatter_enabled / arbiter_active from _hooklib in statusline.py and drop the local frontmatter() parser (keep a thin wrapper only if the statusline needs the full key map vs the boolean). Guard the import the same defensive way the _taskboardlib import already is, so a missing lib never breaks the box.
acceptance_criteria: statusline.py derives arbiter-enabled state from _hooklib rather than a local parser
The local frontmatter() duplicate is removed or reduced to a thin _hooklib delegation
The statusline still renders if the import fails (existing defensive pattern preserved)

title: create.ts hand-rolls raw --mount type=volume argv, bypassing the documented buildMountArgs single-chokepoint
evidence: mounts.ts documents itself as 'the single chokepoint that turns mount specs into docker --mount argv' and 'the one place mount argv is built' (mounts.ts:6-25), and run.ts/cp.ts/claude-inside.ts all route through buildMountArgs. But create.ts constructs --mount argv as raw string literals in two places: buildCloneArgs (178-179: '--mount', `type=volume,source=${volumeName},target=${APP_DIR}`) and defaultBuildImage (212-213, same shape). Neither goes through buildMountArgs. Today these only build named-volume mounts (no user-controlled source), so no bind can leak — the runtime isolation guarantee holds. The defect is structural: the 'single chokepoint' / 'one place' invariant the module advertises is false, and these inline builders are not covered by the bind-rejection guarantee.
impact: The documented invariant ('run.ts / cp.ts ... can never hand docker a bind mount' because mount argv is built in one place) does not actually hold for create.ts. If a future edit parameterizes the clone/build source from less-trusted input, the inline path has no bind guard. Low today because the sources are internally-generated volume names, but it weakens the audit story for the module that is the product's isolation boundary.
recommendation: Route create.ts's clone and build-helper mounts through buildMountArgs([{type:'volume',source:volumeName,target:APP_DIR}]) so there is genuinely one mount-argv path, and update mounts.ts's docstring to list create.ts among the callers (or, if create.ts is intentionally excluded, state why). Either close the gap or correct the invariant claim.
acceptance_criteria: create.ts builds its --mount argv via buildMountArgs rather than string literals
mounts.ts's 'single chokepoint' claim is true for every mount-emitting caller, or documents the explicit exception
Existing ca-sandbox tests pass

title: statusline.py embeds a ~290-LOC stateful cost-ledger subsystem in a render entry-point, against the repo's own thin-entry-point standard
evidence: statusline.py (1286 LOC) holds a self-contained token/cost ledger subsystem (405-693): an Anthropic pricing table (price_for/api_cost), transcript-tailing accumulation (_tx_accumulate, _agg_reqs, _totals), JSON persistence to ~/.codearbiter/ledger.json with TTL pruning and an atomic-write protocol (ledger_update), and a burn sparkline. This has nothing to do with the box-drawing/segment rendering that is the file's nominal job. coding-standards.md:24-27 mandates 'Shared logic goes in a leading-underscore library module ... Keep entry points thin; keep testable logic in the lib' — the ledger is exactly the kind of testable, persistent, drift-prone (pricing changes) logic that standard says belongs in a _*lib.py, yet it lives inline in a main()-gated render script and is not unit-testable in isolation.
impact: The pricing table and dedup/aggregation logic — which must change whenever Anthropic pricing changes and is easy to get subtly wrong — cannot be tested without the rendering harness, and the rendering concerns cannot be reasoned about without scrolling past 290 lines of accounting. The file is the only enforcement-adjacent module that breaks the repo's stated thin-entry-point rule at this scale.
recommendation: Extract the ledger into a _ledgerlib.py (pricing table, accumulation, persistence, sparkline) with its own .github/scripts test, mirroring _metricslib/_taskboardlib. statusline.py imports it for the cost segment. Route as /ca:refactor (behavior-preserving).
acceptance_criteria: The cost-ledger logic lives in a dedicated _*lib.py with zero import-time side effects and a unit test
statusline.py consumes it and is materially thinner
The statusline cost segment renders identically before and after
